questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: Under the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, the 'Deal Value Threshold' (DVT) has been introduced primarily to address which of the following challenges?

options.text: The circumvention of merger regulations by high-value transactions in digital markets that lack significant local assets.
The excessive time taken by the CCI to approve mergers in the manufacturing sector.
The lack of transparency in the valuation of Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) during disinvestment.
The rising dominance of Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) in the retail landscape.

questions.explanation: The Deal Value Threshold (DVT) was introduced to regulate Mergers and Acquisitions (M&As) specifically in digital markets where traditional asset-based thresholds might not capture high-value deals. Transactions exceeding Rs. 2000 crore must now be notified to the CCI.
questions.takeaway: DVT is a mechanism to capture high-value digital deals that might otherwise escape regulatory scrutiny due to low physical asset values.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Deal value threshold (DVT): To regulate Merger & Acquisition (M&As) particularly in the digital markets. All M&A transactions with deal value exceeding Rs. 2000 crore are to be notified to the CCI.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: In the context of Indian competition law, 'Predatory Pricing' is legally identified as:

options.text: A mandatory requirement for new entrants to gain market share in a competitive landscape.
An abuse of dominance by a dominant enterprise under Section 4(2) of the Competition Act, 2002.
A standard pricing strategy used by MSMEs to compete with large conglomerates.
A practice exempted from CCI scrutiny under the Cost Regulations, 2025.

questions.explanation: Section 4(2) of the Competition Act, 2002, specifically identifies predatory pricing by a dominant enterprise as an 'abuse of dominance'. The CCI recently notified Cost Regulations, 2025, to provide new definitions to curb this practice.
questions.takeaway: Predatory pricing is treated as an abuse of market dominance rather than a legitimate competitive strategy under the Competition Act.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Section 4(2) of the Competition Act, 2002 identifies predatory pricing by a dominant enterprise as an abuse of dominance.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 introduced 'Settlement and Commitment Mechanisms'. What is the primary functional advantage of these mechanisms for the Competition Commission of India (CCI)?

options.text: They allow the CCI to bypass the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal in all anti-competitive cases.
They empower the CCI to accept voluntary commitments from parties to resolve cases faster without a full investigation.
They mandate that all M&A transactions must be settled through a fixed fee rather than a percentage of deal value.
They eliminate the need for a Chairperson in the CCI for cases involving digital markets.

questions.explanation: The Settlement and Commitment Mechanisms empower the CCI to accept voluntary commitments from parties involved in certain anti-competitive cases, which streamlines the resolution process and avoids lengthy litigation.
questions.takeaway: Settlement and Commitment mechanisms are designed to streamline enforcement by allowing voluntary resolution of anti-competitive concerns.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Settlement and Commitment Mechanisms: Empowers CCI to accept voluntary commitments from parties in certain anti-competitive cases.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: To discourage frivolous litigation and streamline the appellate process, the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 has introduced which of the following requirements for filing an appeal against a CCI order?

options.text: A mandatory deposit of 10% of the deal value of the transaction under dispute.
A mandatory deposit of 25% of the penalty imposed by the CCI.
Prior approval from the Standing Committee on Finance.
A bank guarantee equivalent to the total annual turnover of the enterprise.

questions.explanation: The 2023 Amendment requires a mandatory deposit of 25% of the imposed penalty to file an appeal against a CCI order, which acts as a deterrent against unnecessary appeals.
questions.takeaway: Appealing a CCI order now requires a significant upfront financial commitment (25% of the penalty).
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Proportional Penalties: To file an appeal against a CCI order, a mandatory deposit of 25% of the imposed penalty is now required.
